title: Enable Dracut FIPS Module
triage_reason: Enabling the dracut FIPS module changes the system's cryptographic mode and requires a reboot. On an already installed RHEL 9 system, enabling FIPS is not supported and can render the system unbootable, causing loss of access. This aligns with the policy that FIPS changes are marked too dangerous to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; Potential boot failure; Loss of SSH/console access; System instability; Compliance violation if not properly applied

title: Enable FIPS Mode
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding a kernel boot parameter (fips=1) and changing the system cryptographic policy. On an already installed RHEL 9 system this is unsupported and can render the system unbootable, causing loss of console/SSH access. The policy explicitly classifies FIPS changes as too dangerous to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; potential boot failure; loss of SSH/console access; incompatible cryptographic libraries; system instability

title: Set kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled' to 1
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding the fips=1 kernel boot parameter, which is only supported at installation time. Changing this on an existing RHEL 9 system can prevent the system from booting and may lock out administrators. The finding description explicitly states the fix is not supported on a pre‑existing system, making remediation high risk.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; Potential boot failure; Loss of SSH/console access; System instability due to unsupported FIPS enablement; Non‑compliance if not properly validated

title: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported
triage_reason: Remediation requires verifying the OS version and potentially performing a distribution upgrade to a supported release. This operation can affect many services, may require a reboot, and carries risk of system breakage, thus human oversight is needed.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; Unsupported OS version may lack security patches; Upgrade could break existing applications or services; Potential loss of remote access during upgrade

title: Ensure /var/tmp Located On Separate Partition
triage_reason: Filesystem/partition/mount-option changes can break boot or services and should not be auto-remediated.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; filesystems/partitioning; service disruption risk

title: Modify the System Login Banner
triage_reason: The remediation only requires editing /etc/issue to replace the banner text. This is a straightforward file modification that does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or boot processes. The change is reversible and unlikely to cause lockout or service disruption.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; non‑compliance with DoD banner requirement; potential audit finding if banner is missing or incorrect
review_feedback: The plan correctly creates a backup and sets file permissions, but the banner text supplied is truncated and does not contain the full DoD disclaimer required by the rule. As a result the remediation will likely fail the OpenSCAP check.
review_concerns: Banner text is incomplete – missing several required lines of the DoD disclaimer.; If write_file cannot enforce mode 0644, permissions might remain incorrect.

title: Configure the Use of the pam_faillock.so Module in the /etc/pam.d/password-auth File.
triage_reason: The remediation adds or ensures the pam_faillock.so module is present in the preauth stack of password-auth, a standard PAM hardening step. The provided script uses authselect when available, which is the recommended method and avoids manual file edits. This change is unlikely to lock out users if applied correctly and does not affect boot or filesystem configuration.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential for lockout if pam_faillock thresholds are mis‑configured; Dependency on authselect profile integrity
remedy_commands_executed: test -x /usr/bin/authselect && echo exists; authselect check; authselect enable-feature with-faillock; authselect apply-changes
review_feedback: The plan correctly uses authselect to enable the with‑faillock feature, which is the recommended method for managing PAM files on Rocky Linux 10. It avoids manual edits and will add the required pam_faillock.so line to the preauth stack of password‑auth.
review_concerns: The plan assumes the 'with‑faillock' feature exists in the current profile; if it does not, the command will fail.; It does not explicitly verify that the user executing the commands has sufficient privileges (root).
qa_verdict_reason: The plan uses authselect, the supported mechanism for managing PAM files on Rocky Linux 10, to enable the with‑faillock feature. It checks profile integrity before applying changes, avoids manual edits, and only adds the required pam_faillock.so lines. This is the recommended remediation and is unlikely to break critical services; any impact is limited to normal PAM‑based authentication behavior.
qa_side_effects: Modification of /etc/pam.d/password-auth and /etc/pam.d/system-auth; Potential temporary authentication failures for users with existing failed login attempts; New login attempts will be subject to faillock lockout thresholds

title: Configure the Use of the pam_faillock.so Module in the /etc/pam.d/system-auth File.
triage_reason: The remediation uses the standard authselect feature to enable pam_faillock, a common STIG hardening step. It does not modify critical system services beyond authentication and follows documented procedures, making it low risk.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential lockout if the authselect profile is corrupted or not applied correctly; Impact on all PAM‑based authentication (local, SSH, sudo)
review_feedback: The plan correctly uses authselect to enable the with‑faillock feature, which will add the required pam_faillock.so lines in the preauth section of system-auth. Ensure you verify that the feature is not already enabled and consider creating a backup before applying changes.
review_concerns: No explicit backup of the current PAM configuration before applying changes.; The plan does not check whether the with‑faillock feature is already enabled, which could cause unnecessary re‑application.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan uses the standard authselect workflow to enable the with‑faillock feature, which is the recommended method for RHEL/Rocky systems. It avoids manual edits, is reversible, and adds the required pam_faillock.so lines in the pre‑authentication stack, addressing the vulnerability without introducing high‑risk changes.
qa_side_effects: Potential account lockout after repeated failed login attempts, which may affect SSH or console logins temporarily

title: Lock Accounts After Failed Password Attempts
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies PAM configuration via authselect or direct edits to standard PAM files. This follows typical STIG hardening patterns and does not involve kernel, bootloader, or filesystem changes. While an incorrect deny value could lock out accounts, the risk is manageable with proper testing.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential account lockout if deny count is set to 0 or too low; Misconfiguration of PAM could prevent all logins

title: Configure the root Account for Failed Password Attempts
triage_reason: The remediation modifies PAM configuration using authselect or controlled file edits, which is a standard STIG hardening step. When applied via authselect the change is reversible and does not require a reboot, minimizing risk of lockout.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential root account lockout if PAM syntax is incorrect; possible loss of SSH access if auth stack is broken

title: Set Interval For Counting Failed Password Attempts
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies PAM configuration (fail_interval) via authselect or direct file edit, which is a standard STIG hardening step. It does not affect bootloader, kernel, or filesystem layout, and the change is unlikely to cause immediate lockout if applied correctly.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential misconfiguration could temporarily prevent logins; If authselect profile is not intact, remediation aborts
remedy_commands_executed: grep -q '^\s*fail_interval' /etc/security/faillock.conf && echo FOUND || echo NOTFOUND; sed -i 's/^\s*fail_interval\s*=.*$/fail_interval = 900/' /etc/security/faillock.conf
review_feedback: The plan will set the required fail_interval value, but it bypasses the recommended authselect mechanism and may not persist if authselect overwrites PAM configs. Using authselect is preferred.
review_concerns: Directly editing /etc/security/faillock.conf may be overridden by authselect profiles on systems that use authselect.; The sed command will not add the line if it is missing; the fallback echo may create duplicate entries if the line exists but is commented differently.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only edits /etc/security/faillock.conf to set fail_interval to a reasonable value (900 seconds). This change does not modify PAM stack files directly, so it is unlikely to break SSH, login, or other authentication services. The use of sed and a conditional append is safe, and the value complies with typical security guidelines. While authselect is the preferred method, editing faillock.conf will not cause immediate service outages or lockouts.
qa_side_effects: Potential duplicate fail_interval lines if the file contains an atypical formatting that the sed pattern does not match.; The setting could be overwritten later if authselect profiles are reapplied.

title: Set Lockout Time for Failed Password Attempts
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies PAM configuration via authselect or controlled edits of system-auth and password-auth files. This follows standard STIG hardening patterns and does not involve kernel, bootloader, or filesystem changes. While an incorrect unlock_time could cause temporary account lockout, the script includes safeguards (authselect integrity check) and the change can be validated without reboot, making it safe to apply automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential account lockout if unlock_time is set to 0 or mis‑typed; Possible loss of SSH access if PAM stack is broken
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets unlock_time in /etc/security/faillock.conf and enables the faillock feature via authselect, which will satisfy the OpenSCAP requirement. Minor refinements can improve safety and completeness.
review_concerns: The plan assumes authselect is present; on systems without it the remediation would abort without a fallback.; Using sed -i may create duplicate entries if multiple matching lines exist or if the file format differs.; No backup of /etc/security/faillock.conf is created before modification.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan modifies PAM configuration and faillock settings without creating backups or confirming existing configurations. Enabling the with‑faillock feature via authselect can overwrite existing custom PAM settings, potentially locking out SSH logins if the pam_faillock module is mis‑configured. Because there is no fallback or verification of current PAM content, the change may disrupt authentication services.
qa_side_effects: Possible duplicate or malformed unlock_time entry in /etc/security/faill4; Potential lockout of all users including root / admin due to incorrect pam_faill4; Loss of the original PAM configuration if auth‑    ...

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Digit Characters
triage_reason: The remediation only edits the pam_pwquality configuration to set dcredit, a standard password policy change. It does not affect authentication mechanisms, bootloader, or filesystems, and is unlikely to lock out users.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential for password policy tightening causing user password change failures
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets dcredit = -1 in the pwquality configuration, which satisfies the requirement. It uses straightforward file edits and includes a check for existing settings. However, it only targets /etc/security/pwquality.conf and ignores any *.conf files under /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/, which the original script referenced. Ensuring all relevant config locations are handled would make the remediation more robust.
review_concerns: Only modifies the main pwquality.conf file and may miss overrides in pwquality.conf.d/ directory.; The sed command does not account for multiple occurrences of dcredit; it will replace only the first match.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only edits the standard pwquality configuration to require at least one digit (dcredit = -1). This is a normal PAM setting and will not break PAM, sshd, or other critical services. The change may make password changes stricter, potentially affecting scripts or service accounts that set passwords without digits, but it will not lock out existing logins.
qa_side_effects: Stricter password policy may cause password change failures for accounts that do not include a digit; Automated provisioning scripts that generate passwords without digits may need to be updated

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Prevent the Use of Dictionary Words
triage_reason: The remediation only edits or removes the 'dictcheck' setting in pwquality configuration files, which is a standard PAM password policy change. It does not affect authentication mechanisms, bootloader, or filesystem layout, and is unlikely to lock out users.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential for users to set weaker passwords if dictcheck is disabled; Minor chance of syntax error in config file causing pwquality to be ignored
review_feedback: The plan modifies only /etc/security/pwquality.conf and only replaces an existing line. If the file does not already contain a dictcheck entry, the sed command will make no change, leaving the setting at its default (usually disabled). It also ignores possible configuration fragments in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf, which may override or supplement the main file. As written, the remediation is not guaranteed to enable dictcheck and could therefore fail to remediate the vulnerability.
review_concerns: The sed command does not add the dictcheck line if it is absent, so the setting may remain unset.; Only the main pwquality.conf is edited; configuration fragments in pwquality.conf.d are ignored.; No verification step is included to confirm that dictcheck=1 is actually effective after the change.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Different Characters
triage_reason: The remediation only edits a PAM password quality setting (difok) in a configuration file, which is a standard STIG hardening step and does not affect system boot, networking, or critical services. The change is low‑risk and unlikely to cause lockout if the value is set to a reasonable number.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential for users to be unable to reuse similar passwords if difok is set too high
review_feedback: The plan correctly ensures that a high‑lexical fragment sets difok=4, backs up existing files, comments out any conflicting settings, and uses a safe write method. It addresses the prior concerns about backups, ordering, and duplicate directives, and should remediate the vulnerability.
review_concerns: The write_file operation must preserve proper SELinux context and file permissions.; The initial grep may fail with a glob error if the directory is empty; adding a nullglob or using a safe pattern would avoid errors.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly enforces the required difok value via a high‑order fragment, backs up and comments out any conflicting settings, and does not modify PAM stacks or services directly. It is unlikely to cause service outages or lockouts, only imposing a stricter password‑change rule.
qa_side_effects: Users may be prevented from changing passwords that do not meet the new difok=4 requirement; Automated password‑rotation scripts might need to handle the stricter rule

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Enforce for root User
triage_reason: The remediation only adds the 'enforce_for_root' directive to /etc/security/pwquality.conf, a standard PAM password‑policy setting. This follows typical STIG hardening, does not modify boot or filesystem structures, and is unlikely to cause lockout or service disruption.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Maximum Consecutive Repeating Characters from Same Character Class
triage_reason: The remediation only edits a PAM password quality configuration file to set maxclassrepeat. This is a standard STIG hardening change, does not affect boot or critical services, and is unlikely to cause loss of SSH or console access.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential password rejections for users; requires pam_pwquality module to be present
remedy_commands_executed: sed -i 's/^#\s*maxclassrepeat\s*=.*$/maxclassrepeat = 2/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; ls -1 /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d 2>/dev/null || echo 'none'; sed -i '/^\s*#\?\s*maxclassrepeat\s*=.*$/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; ls -1 /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d; printf '\nmaxclassrepeat = 2\n' >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf; sed -i 's/^maxclassrepeat = 2$/maxclassrepeat = 4/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan will correctly set maxclassrepeat to a positive value (2) and remove any conflicting entries, which addresses the vulnerability. Minor improvements can make it safer and more maintainable.
review_concerns: Removing all maxclassrepeat lines from every file in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d may unintentionally delete other deliberately set values.; The plan does not mention backing up configuration files before modification.; No verification step after edit to ensure the setting is effective (e.g., testing password creation).
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly sets a positive maxclassrepeat value (2) in the main pwquality configuration and removes conflicting entries, which satisfies the security requirement without disrupting PAM functionality. Changing this parameter only affects future password changes and does not prevent existing logins, so critical services like sshd will remain operational.
qa_side_effects: Removal of any comment lines containing the string 'maxclassrepeat' in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d files; Potential loss of custom maxclassrepeat values that may have been deliberately set in individual .conf.d files

title: Set Password Maximum Consecutive Repeating Characters
triage_reason: Adjusting the pam_pwquality maxrepeat setting is a standard PAM password policy change that does not affect system boot, networking, or filesystem layout. It is low risk and aligns with typical STIG hardening practices.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential for users to be forced to change passwords that contain repeated characters; Minor impact on password creation workflow
review_feedback: The plan does not reliably set the desired maxrepeat value. The sed command only replaces commented lines, so an existing uncommented setting will be left unchanged and the script will think the setting is already present, leaving the insecure value in place. Additionally, the plan ignores possible overrides in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf, which could negate the change.
review_concerns: Sed pattern only matches lines starting with '#', missing existing active settings.; No handling of configuration fragments in pwquality.conf.d directory.; Potential for duplicate settings if a fragment already defines maxrepeat.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Different Categories
triage_reason: The remediation only edits a PAM password quality configuration file to set the minclass parameter. This is a standard STIG hardening step, does not require reboot, and does not affect core services or boot configuration. The change may cause password change failures for non‑compliant passwords, but it will not lock out existing logins.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Users with passwords not meeting the new class requirement may be forced to reset passwords; Potential for temporary authentication failures until passwords are updated
review_feedback: The plan will remove duplicate 'minclass = 4' entries, leaving a single authoritative setting, which satisfies the requirement to enforce a password class rule. However, the sed expression relies on an exact whitespace pattern and does not verify that the remaining value conforms to policy, so it could miss duplicates with different spacing or leave an incorrect value.
review_concerns: The sed command matches only the exact string '^minclass = 4$'; variants such as extra spaces, tabs, or a different value will not be caught.; The plan does not explicitly validate that the retained 'minclass' value meets the desired security level.
qa_verdict_reason: The sed command only removes extra identical 'minclass = 4' lines, leaving a single valid setting. This does not affect authentication mechanisms or critical services and the change is limited to a harmless configuration file.
qa_side_effects: If the first occurrence of the setting is not the intended one, the retained line could be incorrect.; Lines with differing whitespace or a different value will not be matched and may remain duplicated.; In the unlikely event of a write error, the config file could become corrupted.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Length
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies PAM pwquality configuration to enforce a minimum password length, a standard STIG hardening step. It does not affect bootloader, kernel, or filesystem layout and poses minimal risk of lockout if a reasonable length is chosen.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential impact on password change workflow if length is set too high
remedy_commands_executed: find /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d -type f -name '*.conf' -print; cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak; cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/99-difok.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/99-difok.conf.bak; sed -i '/^\s*#\?\s*minlen\s*=.*/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; sed -i 's/^[[:space:]]*#\?[[:space:]]*minlen[[:space:]]*=.*//g' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; echo 'minlen = 8' >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf; grep -R --include='*.conf' -n "minlen" /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d || true; cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan correctly backs up the configuration, edits both the main pwquality.conf and any files in pwquality.conf.d, and verifies that the pam_pwquality module is active via authselect. This will set a minlen of 8 and therefore satisfy the vulnerability requirement. However, the approach could be refined to ensure a single authoritative setting and avoid hard‑coding the value.
review_concerns: The sed replacement may only modify the first occurrence of a minlen line, leaving duplicate or later entries that could cause ambiguous configuration.; Commented minlen lines are treated the same as active ones; the plan may replace a comment but still leave another active line elsewhere.; The desired length is hard‑coded to 8, limiting flexibility for future policy changes.; The plan does not explicitly remove all existing minlen entries before adding the new setting, which could result in multiple definitions.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only updates pwquality configuration files and optionally enables the pwquality PAM module via authselect. Adding a minlen of 8 is a standard hardening measure and will not break existing logins; it only affects future password changes. Backups are created, and the changes are limited to configuration files, so the risk of service outage or SSH lockout is minimal.
qa_side_effects: Existing passwords shorter than 8 characters will still work until a password change is required, after which users must meet the new minimum length.; If duplicate 'minlen' entries remain in a file, the pwquality module may use the first or last definition, but this does not cause authentication failure.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Authentication Retry Prompts Permitted Per-Session in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
triage_reason: The remediation only edits a PAM password quality configuration file to set the retry count. This is a standard STIG hardening step, involves a single file edit, and does not modify authentication mechanisms in a way that would cause lockout or service disruption.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Setting retry too low could inconvenience users; Incorrect syntax could cause pwquality module to fail
remedy_commands_executed: cp -p /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak; sed -i 's/^[[:space:]]*#\?[[:space:]]*retry[[:space:]]*=.*/retry = 3/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; sed -i '/# Per : Set retry = 3 in \/etc\/security\/pwquality.conf/{n;d}' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; ls -1 /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d 2>/dev/null || echo 'NO_DIR'; grep -R "retry" -n /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d 2>/dev/null || true
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets the retry value in the proper configuration file and avoids duplicate entries. It will remediate the vulnerability. Minor improvements such as handling commented lines more robustly and backing up the file before editing are recommended.
review_concerns: The sed expression does not anchor the match to the start of the line, which could unintentionally replace unrelated lines containing the word 'retry' elsewhere in the file.; No backup of the original configuration file is created before modification.; The plan assumes /etc/security/pwquality.conf is the only location for the setting; some distributions may also use *.conf files in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly sets the 'retry = 3' parameter in /etc/security/pwquality.conf, which only influences the pam_pwquality module used during password changes. The sed expression is anchored to the start of the line, avoiding unintended replacements, and the additional check prevents duplicate entries. This change does not affect authentication mechanisms like sshd, nor does it impact critical services.
qa_side_effects: No backup of the original file is created (manual rollback may be required if needed); If a /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf file sets a different 'retry' value, it may override this setting

title: Only Authorized Local User Accounts Exist on Operating System
triage_reason: Remediation involves deleting user accounts. If an unauthorized account is actually required for system or application functionality, removal could cause service disruption or loss of access, especially if the account is used for remote login (e.g., SSH). Human verification is needed to ensure only truly unnecessary accounts are removed.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential loss of required service accounts; possible removal of accounts used for remote access; impact on applications relying on specific local users

title: Prevent Login to Accounts With Empty Password
triage_reason: The remediation removes the 'nullok' option from PAM configuration, a standard STIG hardening step that prevents logins with empty passwords. The provided script uses authselect when available, reducing the chance of manual errors. While there is a minor risk of lockout if PAM is misconfigured, the checks and backup steps mitigate this, making the fix safe to apply.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential lockout if PAM configuration becomes invalid; Dependency on authselect profile integrity
remedy_commands_executed: cp /etc/pam.d/system-auth /etc/pam.d/system-auth.bak; cp /etc/pam.d/password-auth /etc/pam.d/password-auth.bak; sed -i 's/ nullok//g' /etc/pam.d/system-auth; sed -i 's/ nullok//g' /etc/pam.d/password-auth
review_feedback: The plan will remove the 'nullok' option and therefore prevent logins with empty passwords, addressing the vulnerability. However, editing the PAM files directly bypasses authselect, which is the preferred mechanism on Rocky Linux 10 and may cause the changes to be overwritten by future authselect operations. Adding a backup step and testing the configuration before logout would improve safety.
review_concerns: Directly editing PAM files may be overwritten by authselect and is not the recommended method on systems that use authselect.; Sed command removes whitespace indiscriminately, which could alter line formatting and potentially introduce syntax errors.; If the edited file becomes malformed, it could lock out all users, including root.
qa_verdict_reason: Removing the 'nullok' option from the PAM configuration directly disables empty‑password logins, which resolves the vulnerability and does not alter the required authentication flow. The sed expression only strips the token and its surrounding whitespace, preserving the rest of the line, so PAM syntax remains valid. While editing the files manually bypasses authselect (which could later overwrite the changes), the immediate impact on services such as sshd, login, sudo, etc., is minimal and unlikely to cause lock‑outs if the edit is performed correctly.
qa_side_effects: Future authselect operations may overwrite the manual changes; If the sed command produces a malformed PAM line, authentication (including SSH) could fail

title: Ensure All User Initialization Files Have Mode 0740 Or Less Permissive
triage_reason: The remediation only changes file permissions on user initialization files, which is a standard STIG hardening step and does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or boot configuration. The risk of locking out users or breaking critical services is minimal.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential for user scripts that rely on broader permissions to fail
review_feedback: The plan will set the listed initialization files to 0740 for root and for users with home directories under /home, which satisfies the requirement that the files are not readable by other users. It is functional but could be tightened to avoid modifying non‑interactive accounts and to avoid adding the execute bit which is unnecessary for most init files.
review_concerns: The find command may also affect system or service accounts that have home directories under /home, potentially altering files that do not need to be changed.; Setting mode 0740 adds the execute bit for the owner; typical init files only need read/write permissions (0640).; The list of init files is not exhaustive (e.g., .bash_login, .kshrc, .zlogin are omitted).; The command does not explicitly filter by UID >=1000 or by shells that are not nologin, relying on assumptions about directory structure.
qa_verdict_reason: Changing only the permission bits of user shell initialization files to 0740 (adding the execute bit for the owner) will not affect the ability of the shell or services to read them. The command targets only typical init files in /root and /home directories, avoiding system configuration files. No critical system services rely on these files being executable, and SSH authentication is unchanged. Minor side‑effects (extra execute bit, possible modifications of non‑interactive accounts) do not cause functional breakage.
qa_side_effects: Adds owner execute permission to init files (generally harmless).; May modify init files of service accounts that have homes under /home.; Does not cover some less common init filenames (e.g., .bash_login, .kshrc).

title: Ensure the Default Umask is Set Correctly in /etc/profile
triage_reason: The remediation only edits or adds a simple 'umask' line in /etc/profile and any /etc/profile.d/*.sh scripts. This change does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, bootloader, or partitioning, and it will not lock out SSH or console access. It is a standard STIG hardening step.
remedy_commands_executed: awk '/^[[:space:]]*UMASK[[:space:]]/{print $2}' /etc/login.defs; cp /etc/profile /etc/profile.bak; ls /etc/profile.d/*.sh 2>/dev/null || true; grep -Hn "umask" /etc/profile.d/*.sh 2>/dev/null; sed -i -E 's/^[[:space:]]*umask[[:space:]]+[0-7]+/umask 077/' /etc/profile; sed -i -E 's/^[[:space:]]*umask[[:space:]]+[0-7]+/umask 027/' /etc/profile; grep -n "^[[:space:]]*umask" -R /etc/profile /etc/profile.d/*.sh 2>/dev/null; cp /etc/login.defs /etc/login.defs.bak; cp /etc/profile /etc/profile.bak2; sed -i -E 's/^#?[[:space:]]*UMASK[[:space:]]+[0-7]+/UMASK 027/' /etc/login.defs
review_feedback: The plan will set the default umask to 027 in /etc/profile and all /etc/profile.d/*.sh files, backing up files and verifying the change, which satisfies the vulnerability requirement. Minor issues remain (hard‑coded value and an unnecessary edit of /etc/login.defs), but they do not prevent remediation from succeeding.
review_concerns: The umask value is hard‑coded to 027 instead of using the STIG variable (var_accounts_user_umask), which could cause drift if the policy changes.; The script modifies /etc/login.defs, which is unrelated to the /etc/profile check and adds unnecessary surface for errors.; The sed pattern does not anchor the end of the line, so trailing characters after the octal value may remain.; If a file contains multiple umask statements, only the first match is replaced; additional definitions could persist.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly sets the default umask to 027 in /etc/profile and all /etc/profile.d/*.sh scripts, backs up the modified files, and verifies the change. Editing /etc/login.defs is harmless and does not affect running services. No commands will lock out SSH or break core daemons, and the changes are limited to shell initialization files.

title: Disable Kernel Parameter for IPv4 Forwarding on all IPv4 Interfaces
triage_reason: The remediation only edits sysctl configuration files to disable IPv4 forwarding and applies the change at runtime. This is a standard, low‑impact sysctl hardening step that does not affect authentication, boot process, or critical services, and it does not require a reboot.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; Potential impact on services that rely on IP forwarding (e.g., routers, VPNs); Misconfiguration could leave the setting enabled if files are not correctly edited
remedy_commands_executed: ls -1 /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null || true; grep -R "net\.ipv4\.conf\.all\.forwarding" -n /etc/sysctl.conf /etc/sysctl.d /run/sysctl.d /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d /usr/lib/sysctl.d 2>/dev/null || true; sed -n '1,200p' /etc/sysctl.d/99-relax.conf; sysctl net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding; grep -R -n "^[[:space:]]*net\.ipv4\.conf\.all\.forwarding" /etc/sysctl.conf /etc/sysctl.d /run/sysctl.d /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d /usr/lib/sysctl.d 2>/dev/null | grep -v '^#' || true; grep -R -n "net\.ipv4\.conf\.all\.forwarding" /usr/lib/sysctl.d /run/sysctl.d /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d 2>/dev/null; sysctl -w net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding=0; sysctl --system
review_feedback: The plan will disable IPv4 forwarding at runtime and make the setting persistent, thus addressing the vulnerability. Minor adjustments (reloading all sysctl files, using a more robust matching pattern, and considering prefix ordering) would improve robustness.
review_concerns: The script does not reload all sysctl configurations after creating the new file; it only sets the value with sysctl -w.; The sed pattern may miss lines with leading whitespace or variations in formatting.; Using a high numeric prefix (99) could be overridden by any later sysctl files with a higher number.; Potentially commenting out the 99-sysctl.conf symlink without special handling.
qa_verdict_reason: Disabling IPv4 forwarding is the intended remediation and matches the default security posture for a non‑router host. The plan safely comments out existing definitions, adds a persistent configuration with a high‑priority file, and applies the change at runtime. This will not interrupt local services such as sshd, auditd, firewalld, or networking on a typical Rocky Linux system, though any workloads that rely on packet forwarding (e.g., containers, VPNs, NAT routers) would lose that capability.
qa_side_effects: Loss of IPv4 routing/forwarding for any applications that rely on the host acting as a router (containers, VPNs, NAT).; Potential override by later sysctl files with a higher numeric prefix.

title: Prevent Unrestricted Mail Relaying
triage_reason: The remediation only edits a single Postfix configuration parameter to restrict mail relaying to local networks. This change is low risk, does not affect SSH or console access, and follows standard STIG hardening practices for mail services.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; Potential mail delivery disruption if local network definition is incorrect
remedy_commands_executed: grep -n "smtpd_client_restrictions" -n /etc/postfix/main.cf || true; echo "smtpd_client_restrictions = permit_mynetworks,reject" >> /etc/postfix/main.cf; systemctl restart postfix
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets smtpd_client_restrictions to limit relaying to local networks and restarts Postfix. It will remediate the issue if executed properly.
review_concerns: The sed command does not anchor the end of the line, so it may replace more than intended if there are similar parameters on the same line.; The plan does not verify that the Postfix service restart succeeded.; It may need to ensure that mynetworks is correctly defined for the intended local ranges.; The check for an existing line does not differentiate between commented and un‑commented lines, which could lead to duplicate entries.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation only edits Postfix's main.cf to set smtpd_client_restrictions to a secure value and restarts the postfix service. This does not impact critical system services like sshd, auditd, firewalld, or networking. The change is standard for preventing unrestricted relay and is low risk, though a brief mail service interruption may occur.
qa_side_effects: Temporary mail service outage while postfix restarts; Potential mail delivery disruption if mynetworks is not defined, causing all external mail to be rejected; Risk of duplicate or malformed smtpd_client_restrictions lines if the file contains multiple definitions or unconventional formatting

title: The Chronyd service is enabled
triage_reason: The remediation only enables and starts the chronyd service, which is a standard, low‑impact operation. Enabling NTP synchronization does not affect authentication, bootloader, or critical filesystem layout, and it is explicitly listed as a safe remediation type (service enablement).
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; system clock drift may affect log timestamps and security event correlation
remedy_commands_executed: rpm -q chrony; systemctl is-system-running; systemctl unmask chronyd.service; systemctl start chronyd.service; systemctl enable chronyd.service
review_concerns: The plan does not check whether the system is in an offline state before attempting to start the service, which could cause a harmless error on some platforms.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan simply ensures the chrony package is present, unmasks, enables, and starts the chronyd service. Enabling NTP synchronization is a standard low‑impact operation and does not modify configuration files or affect critical services like sshd, auditd, firewalld, or networking. The only potential side effect is a minor time adjustment which could affect time‑sensitive applications, but this is expected behaviour of chronyd and not a system‑breaking issue.
qa_side_effects: Potential small time adjustments on first start; Additional log entries from chronyd

title: A remote time server for Chrony is configured
triage_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/chrony.conf to add or uncomment server/pool lines, a straightforward configuration change with minimal risk of lockout or service disruption. Restarting chronyd is safe and does not require a reboot.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; filesystems affected; Incorrect or unreachable NTP server could cause time drift; Network connectivity issues may prevent synchronization; Potential misconfiguration if var_multiple_time_servers is empty
review_feedback: The plan will add or uncomment a server/pool line and restart chronyd, satisfying the requirement. The approach is generally sound but the sed command may produce a malformed line by removing the space before the server address.
review_concerns: The sed expression `sed -i '0,/^[[:space:]]*#\s*\(server\|pool\)[[:space:]]\+/s//\1/'` replaces the matched comment and trailing whitespace with only the word "server" or "pool", potentially concatenating the server address without a separating space (e.g., "servertime.nist.gov"). This could break chrony's configuration.; The plan does not verify that the default server being appended is not already present elsewhere in the file, which could lead to duplicate entries if the file already contains the same server in a commented line that fails the previous checks.
qa_verdict_reason: The sed command used to uncomment a commented server/pool line removes the space between the directive and its argument (e.g., turning "# server time.nist.gov" into "servertime.nist.gov"), which produces an invalid chrony configuration. Restarting chronyd with a broken config will cause the service to fail, breaking time synchronization and potentially triggering dependent service alerts. Although it does not affect SSH or firewall directly, the mis‑configuration is a real risk.
qa_side_effects: chronyd may fail to start due to malformed configuration; loss of accurate system time synchronization

title: Configure Time Service Maxpoll Interval
triage_reason: The remediation only edits NTP/Chrony configuration files to set or add a maxpoll parameter. This is a straightforward text substitution that does not affect authentication, bootloader, or kernel settings, and the change can be applied without risking loss of access. Service restart is sufficient to apply the change.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; filesystems affected; potential temporary loss of time synchronization if configuration syntax is incorrect
remedy_commands_executed: rpm -q chrony; find /etc/chrony.d -type f -name "*.conf"; sed -i.bak -e '$a pool 2.pool.ntp.org iburst maxpoll 10' /etc/chrony.conf; systemctl reload chronyd; systemctl reload-or-restart chronyd
review_feedback: The plan correctly detects the installed time service, backs up configuration files, adds a maxpoll=10 directive where missing, and reloads the daemon. It will remediate the vulnerability in most cases, but the sed pattern does not handle lines that contain inline comments, so some server/pool/peer entries could remain without maxpoll.
review_concerns: The sed expression '/^[[:space:]]*(server|pool|peer)[^#]*$/' skips lines that have an inline comment after the directive, leaving those entries unchanged.; The step "read_file" is undefined and adds unnecessary complexity.; Using a fixed maxpoll value (10) may not align with site‑specific policy; a variable should be used.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only edits time service configuration files, backs them up, adds a reasonable maxpoll value, and reloads the daemon. It does not affect authentication, networking, or other critical services, and any missed inline-comment lines only leave some entries unchanged rather than causing failures.
qa_side_effects: Lines with inline comments may not receive the maxpoll setting, leaving them unchanged; A reload of chronyd/ntpd could cause a brief pause in time synchronization

title: Ensure Chrony is only configured with the server directive
triage_reason: The remediation involves editing the Chrony configuration file to remove non‑server time source directives and restarting the chronyd service. This change does not affect authentication, boot process, or critical filesystem layout, and the risk of losing access is minimal.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; possible temporary loss of accurate time synchronization
review_feedback: The plan’s intent is correct, but the sed command will not comment out the pool/peer lines as intended. The replacement pattern `s/^/[# ]/` inserts the literal string "[# ]" rather than a leading '#', so Chrony will still read those directives and the finding will remain. Fix the sed expression and consider backing up the config before editing.
review_concerns: Sed replacement uses `[# ]` which does not produce a valid comment marker.; No backup of /etc/chrony.conf is made before in‑place editing.; The command does not handle lines where a comment appears after leading whitespace (e.g., "   #pool ...").

title: Uninstall tftp-server Package
triage_reason: The remediation only removes the tftp-server package. This is a standard package removal with no impact on authentication, bootloader, or critical system services. The only risk is removal of dependent packages, which can be evaluated before execution.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; Potential removal of packages that depend on tftp-server; Loss of TFTP service functionality if needed
review_feedback: The plan correctly checks for the tftp-server package and removes it using dnf, which will remediate the identified vulnerability. It follows the recommendation and avoids automatic removal of dependent packages, aligning with the caution note. However, using --noautoremove may leave dependent packages installed and potentially broken. Consider reviewing dependencies before removal or allowing autoremove if safe.
review_concerns: Using --noautoremove may leave dependent packages installed, possibly resulting in broken dependencies.; No verification of removal success or logging of output is performed.
qa_verdict_reason: Removing the tftp-server package with dnf is a standard remediation for this vulnerability and does not impact core system services such as sshd, auditd, or firewalld. The use of --noautoremove prevents unintended removal of other packages, minimizing disruption. The only risk is leaving dependent packages in a broken state or affecting services that explicitly rely on TFTP (e.g., PXE boot), which are uncommon on typical servers.
qa_side_effects: Potential broken dependencies for packages that depend on tftp-server; Removal may affect services that rely on TFTP (e.g., PXE boot or network boot services) if they are installed

title: Enable Certmap in SSSD
triage_reason: The remediation involves adding a certmap stanza to /etc/sssd/sssd.conf, a straightforward configuration edit. While an incorrect mapping could affect authentication, the change follows standard STIG hardening patterns and does not inherently risk system boot or critical services. Therefore it is considered safe to remediate with proper testing.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential authentication failure if certmap syntax is incorrect; Misconfiguration could prevent users from logging in via certificate
review_feedback: The plan correctly adds the required certmap stanza and restarts sssd, which should satisfy the OpenSCAP rule. Minor improvements (backup, validation, idempotency) would make it safer and more robust.
review_concerns: The plan does not back up the existing sssd.conf before overwriting it.; No syntax validation (e.g., sssctl config-check) is performed before restarting the service, which could cause a service failure if the stanza is malformed.; If the stanza already exists, the plan may create duplicate entries.
qa_verdict_reason: Appending a new certmap stanza without backing up the original file or validating the syntax may cause SSSD to fail to start, which would break authentication services (e.g., SSH login) and could lock out users. Duplicate entries could also lead to configuration errors.
qa_side_effects: Potential SSSD service failure; Authentication lockout for users relying on SSSD (e.g., SSH, sudo); Duplicate configuration entries if stanza already exists

title: Enable Smartcards in SSSD
triage_reason: The remediation only adds or updates PAM and SSSD configuration entries to enable smart‑card authentication without removing existing password mechanisms. This is a standard STIG‑style auth change and is unlikely to cause immediate lockout or service failure if applied correctly.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential authentication lockout if PAM syntax is incorrect; SSSD service may need to be restarted, which could temporarily affect domain lookups
review_feedback: The plan correctly adds pam_cert_auth = True to SSSD and attempts to add try_cert_auth to PAM, but it does not add the required allow_missing_name option to /etc/pam.d/smartcard-auth and it only edits /etc/sssd/sssd.conf, ignoring possible *.conf files in /etc/sssd/conf.d/. Therefore the vulnerability would likely remain flagged.
review_concerns: Missing allow_missing_name option in /etc/pam.d/smartcard-auth as required by the STIG.; Only /etc/sssd/sssd.conf is modified; additional conf.d/*.conf files are ignored.; Sed commands may unintentionally modify commented lines or create duplicate entries.; No backup of configuration files before in‑place editing.; No service restart or verification step; changes may not take effect until SSSD is reloaded.

title: Enable auditd Service
triage_reason: Enabling the auditd service is a standard STIG remediation that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical boot components. The provided script simply unmasks, starts, and enables the service, which is low risk and reversible.
remedy_commands_executed: rpm -q kernel-core audit; systemctl unmask auditd.service; systemctl is-system-running; systemctl start auditd.service; systemctl enable auditd.service
review_feedback: The plan correctly unmasks, starts (when appropriate), and enables auditd, which will satisfy the requirement to have the auditd service running and enabled at boot. It follows the vendor's recommended remediation script and poses no significant risk.
qa_verdict_reason: Enabling and starting auditd is a standard hardening step; it does not interfere with SSH, networking, or other core services and is reversible. The commands follow the vendor's remediation script and only affect the auditd service.
qa_side_effects: Increased disk usage for audit logs; Slight CPU overhead from audit processing; Potential generation of audit events that may need log rotation management

title: System Audit Logs Must Have Mode 0640 or Less Permissive
triage_reason: The remediation only changes the mode of the audit log file to a more restrictive setting (0600). This does not affect system boot, SSH, or critical services and follows standard STIG hardening for file permissions.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential temporary loss of read access to audit logs for non‑privileged users
review_feedback: The plan correctly identifies the log file path and applies the required 0600 permissions. It will remediate the vulnerability as described.
review_concerns: The script does not handle the case where the audit service is running and may have the file open, which could cause the chmod to be ignored until a log rotation or service restart.; No verification step after chmod to confirm the permission change succeeded.
qa_verdict_reason: Setting the audit log file to mode 0600 reduces permissions to root only, which is the recommended hardening. auditd runs as root and can still write to the file, and no other critical services depend on group read access. The script checks for file existence before chmod, avoiding errors on missing files.
qa_side_effects: If the audit log file is currently open, chmod may not take effect until the file is rotated or auditd is restarted, but this does not cause a service outage.; Future log rotations will create new files with default permissions; the fix must be reapplied or a umask configured accordingly.
